revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Hioka-jinja]][[ja:日岡神社]] {{Expand Japanese|日岡神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Hioka Shrine | native_name = 日岡神社 | image = Hioka-jinja gaihaiden.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Hioka Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|34.780464|134.86028}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Ame no Hisasahiko no Mikoto|lt=Ame no Hisasahiko no Mikoto|qid=Q112367505|1=Ame no Hisasahiko no Mikoto}} }} {{nihongo|'''Hioka Shrine'''|日岡神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Harima Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Kokuhei-sha|lt=Kokuhei-sha|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database * {{ill|Q135026601|ltq=Q135026601|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Harima Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Harima Province|ja|播磨国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11498723|1=List of Shikinaisha in Harima Province}} ** Series ordinal: 8 ** P155: {{ill|Q135041149|ltq=Q135041149|qid=Q135041149|1=Iwatsuhimeno Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Q11458547|ltq=Q11458547|qid=Q11458547|1=Itatehyōzu Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|lt=Kakogawa|ar|كاكوغاوا (هيوغو)|azb|کاکوقاوا، هیوقو|az|Kakoqava|bg|Какогава|ceb|Kakogawachō-honmachi|ce|Какогава|cs|Kakogawa (Hjógo)|de|Kakogawa|en|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|eo|Kakogava|es|Kakogawa|et|Kakogawa|fa|کاکوگاوا، هیوگو|fi|Kakogawa|fr|Kakogawa|ga|Kakogawa|gl|Kakogawa|it|Kakogawa|ja|加古川市|ko|가코가와시|lt|Kakogava|mg|Kakogawachō-honmachi|mzn|هاکوگاوا، هیوگو|nl|Kakogawa|no|Kakogawa|pl|Kakogawa|pt|Kakogawa|ro|Kakogawa|ru|Какогава|sh|Kakogava|simple|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|sr|Какогава|sv|Kakogawa|tg|Какогава|th|คาโกงาวะ|tl|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|tr|Kakogawa|tt|Какогава|uk|Какоґава|ur|کاکوگاوا، ہیوگو|uz|Kakogawa|vi|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|war|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|zh_classical|加古川市|zh_min_nan|Kakogawa-chhī|zh_yue|加古川|zh|加古川市|qid=Q750569|1=Kakogawa, Hyōgo}} * {{ill|Q1047523|ltq=Q1047523|qid=Q1047523|1=Harima Province}} * {{ill|Kako District, Hyōgo|lt=Kako district|ceb|Kako-gun|en|Kako District, Hyōgo|es|Distrito de Kako|fr|District de Kako|id|Distrik Kako, Hyōgo|it|Distretto di Kako|ja|加古郡|ko|가코군|pl|Powiat Kako|ru|Како (уезд)|zh|加古郡|qid=Q1042066|1=Kako District, Hyōgo}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|34.780464|134.86028}} == image (P18) == * Hioka-jinja gaihaiden.JPG == dedicated to (P825) == * {{ill|Ame no Hisasahiko no Mikoto|lt=Ame no Hisasahiko no Mikoto|qid=Q112367505|1=Ame no Hisasahiko no Mikoto}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 183014 ** P3831: {{ill|Q135159299|ltq=Q135159299|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q134917316|ltq=Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == name in kana (P1814) == * ひおかじんじゃ == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 5140005009431 == Commons category (P373) == * Hioka-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県加古川市加古川町大野字日岡山1755', 'language': 'ja'} == Japan Search name ID (P6698) == * 日岡神社 {{commons category|Category:Hioka-jinja}} [[JA:日岡神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11505275}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Hioka Shrine |Image = [[File:Hioka-jinja gaihaiden.JPG|260px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|qid=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kakogawa City|Q750569|qid=Q750569|1=Kakogawa, Hyōgo}}1755 Hiokayama, Ono, Kakogawa-machi |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Tenisasahiko no Mikoto |Divinity = |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} (small)
Old {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Foundation = unknown
(One theory is {{ill|lt=Tenpyo|Q1199879|qid=Q1199879|1=Tenpyō}}2 years ({{ill|lt=730 years|Q23563|qid=Q23563|1=730}})) |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular festival = 2nd Saturday and Sunday in October |Shinto ritual = Inami basket (February) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Hioka Shrine|position=top|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }}'''Hioka Shrine'' (Hioka Shrine) is located in Ono, Kakogawa Town, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|qid=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kakogawa City|Q750569|qid=Q750569|1=Kakogawa, Hyōgo}}. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, {{ill|lt=Modern company rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Overview === It is located at the southern foot of the hill on the left bank of {{ill|lt=Kakogawa|Q11398501|qid=Q11398501|1=Kako River (Japan)}} in southern Hyogo Prefecture. This hill has been called ``Hioka'' since ancient times, and it is also related to the origin of the area's place name ``Kago.'' The ``God of Hioka'' can be found in ``{{ill|lt=Harima Province Fudoki|Q11498726|qid=Q11498726|1=Harima no Kuni Fudoki}}'' of {{ill|lt=Nara Period|Q189178|qid=Q189178|1=Nara period}}, making it an old shrine that has been worshiped since ancient times. Currently, the enshrined deity Tenisasahiko-no-Mikoto is {{ill|lt=Harima Inahi-Taro-hime|Q11078587|qid=Q11078587|lt=Harima Inahi-Taro-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Harima no Inabi no Ōiratsume}} (the 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}{{ill|lt=Empress|Q7723211|qid=Q7723211|1=Empress consort}}) is widely worshiped in the Higashiharima area as the god of safe childbirth, based on a shrine tradition that says that he prayed for a safe childbirth.According to a legend from the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the construction project of {{ill|lt=Kusatani River #Gogai Irrigation|Q24874726|qid=Q24874726|lt=Gogai Irrigation|1=Kusatani RIver}}, the main irrigation water on the left bank of the Kako River, was carried out jointly by Hyuga Myojin (the deity of Hioka) and Prince Shotoku|Q263972}}. This was from the 1300s to the 1310s at the end of the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|qid=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, and was founded by {{jalink|Shingon Risshu|Q580527}, who later became a confidant of {{ill|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|Q313942|qid=Q313942|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. }} (Shingon Ritsu sect has a deep-rooted belief in Prince Shotoku) There is a theory that the construction work carried out by {{ill|lt=Monkan|Q11500110|qid=Q11500110|lt=Monkanbo Hiroma|1=Monkan-bō Kōshin}}, a monk of {{ill|lt=Shingon sect|Q550182|qid=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}}, has become legendary. It is also said that one of Bunkan's grandfathers was a priest at Hioka Shrine. === Enshrined God === The following five deities are currently enshrined. . ; Main enshrined deity :* '''Amenoisasahiko no Mikoto'' ; Deity enshrined :* {{ill|lt=Toyota Mahime|Q2329261|qid=Q2329261|lt=Toyota Hime Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} (Toyota Hime Mikoto) :* {{jalink | :* {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Omikami}} :* {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|qid=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime no Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} (Ichikishimahime no Mikoto)Historical changes can be seen in the deities enshrined at Hioka Shrine {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. First of all, in ancient times, "{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Harima Province Fudoki|1=UNKNOWN}}'' In the Kako Gunjo, the god who sits in Hioka is written as ``Ihatsuhiko no Mikoto'', the son of Oomitsuha no Mikoto.``New Complete Works of Japanese Classical Literature'' 5 Fudoki” Shogakukan, 2003 (Japan Knowledge edition), pp. 18-19. {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. On the other hand, {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} There is only one enshrined deity listed in the book, and in the same book it is written as ``Hioka Zaten Isahiko Shrine'', that is, ``Ama Isahiko Shrine enshrined in Hioka (place name)'', and the enshrined deity is said to be ``Ama Isahiko God'' {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Gods)|1984}}.In "{{ill|lt=Harimakan|Q11498749|qid=Q11498749|1=Harima Kagami}}" written in {{ill|lt=Horeki|Q1194397|qid=Q1194397|1=Hōreki}}12 ({{ill|lt=1762|Q7652|qid=Q7652|1=1762}}), there is a {{jalink|Tamayori bime} in the main hall. (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|Tamayorihime no Mikoto}}, Takanosabuki Fuai no Mikoto on the left, Isazahiko no Mikoto on the right (the origin tradition is { (See section {jalink|#Soken||Soken}}), after which it returned to Tenisasahiko no Mikoto, which appears in the above-mentioned ``Engishiki'' {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. This "Ten Isahiko" is a god that does not appear in classical historical materials, and the ancient documents at Hioka Shrine were destroyed in a military fire in {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}6 ({{ill|lt=1578|Q6707|qid=Q6707|1=1578}}), so its origin is not clear {{Sfn|Hioka Zaten Isahiko Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1980}}. One theory compares Amaisa Sahiko to Hiko Isasa Serihiko (real name of {{ill|lt=Kibitsuhiko-no-Mikoto|Q65318|qid=Q65318|1=Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto}}), and writes "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" {{j alink|Emperor Korei|Q313041}} There is a theory that links it with the legend of the dispatch of Kibitsuhiko, which appears in the diary, but it is not certain as there are also theories that question this {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Gods)|1984}}.In addition, in recent shrine legends, {{ill|lt=Harima Inahitarohime|Q11078587|qid=Q11078587|lt=Harima Inahitarohime no Mikoto|1=Harima no Inabi no Ōiratsume}} (Harima no Inabi no Oiratsume no Mikoto: the 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} Empress) When {jalink|Ousu no Mikoto}} and Ousu no Mikoto ({{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|1=Yamato Takeru}}) and others gave birth to twins, the enshrined deity Tenisasahiko no Mikoto is said to have prayed for a safe delivery, and is now worshiped as the god of safe childbirth.. === History === === Founding === Founding date is unknown. According to the shrine's legend, when {{ill|lt=Emperor Jinmu|Q200188|qid=Q200188|1=Emperor Jimmu}} was on a conquest to the east, Arashenjin caused evil deeds and caused trouble, but due to a plan by Isazabe no Mikoto of {{ill|lt=Kunitsujin|Q60995523|qid=Q60995523|1=Kunitsukami}}, he was able to get rid of Arashenjin. However, Isazabe-no-Mikoto was killed in battle, so the Emperor assigned Isazabe-no-Mikoto to the two pillars of prayer for victory in battle, Tamayorihime and Kusafuai-no-Mikoto (Emperor Jinmu's parents), and enshrined these three pillars. Suppose this is the case. On the other hand, although the basis for this is not clear, there is a theory that it was founded in {{ill|lt=Tenpyo|Q1199879|qid=Q1199879|1=Tenpyō}}2 ({{ill|lt=730|Q23563|qid=Q23563|1=730}}). . [[File:Hiokaryo Kofun haisho.JPG|thumb|240px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Hiokaryo Kofun|Q11505277|qid=Q11505277|1=Hioka Goryo Kofun}} ({{ill|lt=Harima Inahidarohime|Q11078587|qid=Q11078587|lt=Harima Inahitarohime Mikoto|1=Harima no Inabi no Ōiratsume}}Ryo)}}]]The precincts are located at the southern foot of Mt. Hioka, and it is known that Mt. Hioka is home to a group of kofun from the early to middle {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|qid=Q459939|1=Kofun period}} period (Hioka kofun group/Hiokayama kofun group). Among them, the most representative one is {{ill|lt=Hiokaryo Tumulus|Q11505277|qid=Q11505277|1=Hioka Goryo Kofun}} ({{ill|lt=keyhole-shaped tumulus|Q11268718|qid=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, mound length approximately 85.5 meters). There is a theory that it is associated with the ``hirehaka'' found in the Kako-gun section of ``Harima no Kuni Fudoki,'' and based on this theory, it is currently designated as the tomb of Harima Inahitarohime no Mikoto, the empress of Emperor Keiko. === Outline === In the literature, as mentioned above, in the Kako-gun-jo section of ``{{ill|lt=Harima Province Fudoki|Q11498726|qid=Q11498726|1=Harima no Kuni Fudoki}},'' which was established around the first year of {{ill|lt=Reiki|Q1373115|qid=Q1373115|1=Reiki (era)}} ({{ill|lt=715|Q23528|qid=Q23528|1=715}}), there is a reference to the deity enshrined in Hioka {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. {{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|qid=Q30470|1=927}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} link|Harima Province|Q1047523}}{{ill|lt=Kago District|Q1042066|qid=Q1042066|lt=Kago District|1=Kako District, Hyōgo}}, it is written as "Hioka Zaten Isahiko Shrine" and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1999}}. In {{ill|lt=Tensho|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}6 ({{ill|lt=1578|Q6707|qid=Q6707|1=1578}}), it is said that the shrine building and old records were destroyed by military fire, but the situation before then is not known. After it was written as ``Hyuga Daimyojin'' in ``Minesoki'' ({{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|qid=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}-{{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}), the name ``Hinata'' was also used as the company name.{{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Kadokawa)|1988}}.In the {{ill|lt=Early modern period|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}6 ({{ill|lt=1601|Q6725|qid=Q6725|1=1601}}), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Terumasa Ikeda|1=UNKNOWN}}, a ban was issued regarding Hyuga Shrine in the 7th year of the Keicho era ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|qid=Q6727|1=1602}}){{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1999}}. Also, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiwa|Q1146161|qid=Q1146161|1=Meiwa}} ({{ill|lt=1764|Q7659|qid=Q7659|1=1764}}), the shrine building was rebuilt by the people of Ono Village, Kawahara Village, and Mizoguchi Village.{{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Heibonsha)|1999}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|qid=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in February of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}7 ({{ill|lt=1874|Q7761|qid=Q7761|1=1874}}) {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Magazine Materials|1912}} ranked as {{ill|lt=Gosha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} in {jalink|Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534}}. The shrine building was renovated and rebuilt in the 3rd year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|qid=Q7741|1=1870}}), but in 1944 ({{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}) It was destroyed in a fire in 1969|Q2485}}, and the current shrine building was rebuilt in 1969 ({{ill|lt=1971|Q2475|qid=Q2475|1=1971}}){{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. The name of the shrine was called ``Shoichii Hyuga Daimyojin'' in the early modern period, and was renamed ``Hioka Shrine'' after the Meiji period, but it is still commonly known locally as ``Hinata-san,'' ``Ohno-san,'' and ``Hioka-san.'' === Gogai construction legend === According to the legend in the "Gogai origin story" written in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}} (composed around October 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1657|Q6987|qid=Q6987|1=1657}})), the lower reaches of the {{ill|lt=Kakogawa|Q11398501|qid=Q11398501|1=Kako River (Japan)}} {{ill|lt=Kusatani River#Gogai Irrigation|Q24874726|qid=Q24874726|lt=Gogai Irrigation|1=Kusatani RIver}}, which is the backbone of the left bank of the area, is said to have been built by Hyuga Myojin (the deity of Hioka Shrine) and {{ill|lt=Prince Shotoku|Q263972|qid=Q263972|1=Prince Shōtoku}} in unison.{{sfn|Kaneko|2019|pp=13–15}}. Because the agreement was made at the ``Aragase Futsubashi Bridge'' near {{ill|lt=Hiokaryo Tumulus|Q11505277|qid=Q11505277|lt=Hyugayama|1=Hioka Goryo Kofun}}, it is said that this bridge came to be called by another name, ``Dango Bridge'' {{sfn|Kaneko|2019|pp=13–15}}.Japanese history researcher Tetsu Kaneko was a monk of the {{ill|lt=Shingon Ritsu sect|Q580527|qid=Q580527|1=Shingon Risshu}} and {{ill|lt=Shingon sect|Q550182|qid=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} who was active from the end of the {{ill|lt=13th century|Q7049|qid=Q7049|1=13th century}} to the middle of the {{ill|lt=14th century|Q7034|qid=Q7034|1=14th century}}. {{ill|lt=Emperor Godaigo|Q313942|qid=Q313942|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}'s confidant {{ill|lt=Monkan|Q11500110|qid=Q11500110|lt=Monkanbo Hiroma|1=Monkan-bō Kōshin}} suggests that one of his grandfathers may have been a priest at Hioka Shrine.{{sfn|Kaneko|2019|p=22}}. Additionally, the Gogai Canal Repair Project, which is thought to have been carried out during this period, was carried out by Bunkan from the 1300s to the 1310s at Saidaiji Temple in Nara, the headquarters of the Shingon Ritsu sect. (Nara City)|Q1203935|In addition to support from Saidaiji Temple}}, it is claimed that they received help from local forces such as Hioka Shrine.{{sfn|Kaneko|2019|pp=13–15}}. It is also speculated that the story of ``Gokai Originki'' is a legendary remnant of the memory that the power of Hioka Shrine and the power of Saidaiji Temple, a Shingon Risshu sect that had a strong faith in Prince Shotoku, jointly carried out construction projects through Bunkan {{sfn|Kaneko|2019|pp=13–15}}. === Precincts === [[File:Hioka-jinja shaden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Shrine building}}{{small|Main shrine, inner worship hall, etc. }}]] The shrine building was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}46 ({{ill|lt=1971|Q2475|qid=Q2475|1=1971}}). It is {{ill|lt=Gongenzukuri|Q3155268|qid=Q3155268|1=Ishi-no-ma-zukuri}} made of reinforced concrete. File:Hioka-jinja shinmon.JPG|Zushinmon File:Hioka-jinja, otorii.jpg|Otorii === Setsusha === [[File:Hioka-jinja Otorii-jinja.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Iyagawara Hioka Shrine
(Otorii Shrine)}}]] * Iyagawara Hioka Shrine (Otorii Shrine) - Enshrined deity: Amano Sasahiko no Mikoto. In July 1971, Iyagawara Hioka Shrine, located in Iyagawara, Jike-cho, Kakogawa-cho, Kakogawa City, was relocated. It is larger than other precinct companies. * Takamiki Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Oonamuchi|UNKNOWN|lt=Otomi Takamiko}}, {{ill|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}. Enshrined at the rear right facing the headquarters. The shrine building was built in drift construction. * Tenma Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|UNKNOWN|lt=Shoname Bikona no Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|qid=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}. It is located on the left side facing the headquarters. The shrine building was built in drift construction. * Ebisu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Hiruko|Q1067028|qid=Q1067028|lt=Ebisu no Mikoto|1=Hiruko}}. It is located on the left side facing the headquarters. The shrine building is Shinmei-zukuri. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|UNKNOWN|lt=Hoshokujin}}. It is enshrined on the left side facing the head office. The shrine building was built in drift construction. * Sumiyoshi Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi God|UNKNOWN|lt=Uezutsuo no Mikoto, Nakazutsuo no Mikoto, Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto}}. A stone shrine located near the entrance to the temple grounds. * Kumano Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanamison|1=Izanagi}}, {{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanamison|1=Izanami}}. A stone shrine located near the entrance to the temple grounds. File:Hioka-jinja Takamikura-jinja.JPG|Takamikura ShrineFile:Hioka-jinja Ebisu-jinja and Tenman-jinja.JPG|Tenma Shrine (left) and Ebisu Shrine (right) File:Hioka-jinja Inari-jinja.JPG|Inari Shrine File:Hioka-jinja Kumano-jinja and Sumiyoshi-jinja.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine (left) and Kumano Shrine (right) === Festival === The annual festivals held at Hioka Shrine are as follows [http://www.hiokajinja.or.jp/event.html Annual Events] (official website). . * Monthly ** Monthly Festival (1st of every month) * January ** Saitan Festival (January 1st) ** Genshi Festival (January 3rd) ** Tondo Festival (January 15th) *February ** Hatsuuma Festival ** Festival to ward off evil spirits ** '''Imigomori'' **: Special ritual. It is said that this ritual originates from Tenisasahiko-no-Mikoto shutting herself up for seven days as a prayer for the safe delivery of Inahime Tarohime (another theory is that this ritual originates from the enshrining festival of the two pillars of Tamayori-hime and Kusa-Fuga-no-Mikoto{{Sfn|Hioka Zaten Isahiko Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1980}}). It is held from the Pig Day to the Snake Day of the New Year in the lunar calendar. During this period, noise is prohibited at the shrine, and on the Snake Day, which marks the end of the month of mourning, a Shinto ritual is held. {{Sfn|Hioka Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. ** Prayer Festival (February 17th) * June ** Natsukoshi Festival (Great Purification Ceremony) (last Saturday and Sunday in June) * October ** '''Regular Festival''' (2nd Saturday and Sunday in October) *November ** Shichigosan Shrine *December ** Great Purification Ceremony (December 31st) === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|qid=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kakogawa City|Q750569|qid=Q750569|1=Kakogawa, Hyōgo}}1755 Hiokayama, Ono, Kakogawa-machi '''Access''' * Railway: {{ill|lt=West Japan Railway Company|Q502125|qid=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} (JR West Japan) {{ill|lt=Kakogawa Line|Q874467|qid=Q874467|1=Kakogawa Line}} {{ill|lt=Hioka Station|Q3095774|qid=Q3095774|1=Hioka Station}} (about 6 minutes walk) * Car: Parking available '''Surroundings''' * Hiokayama Tumulus Group ** {{ill|lt=Hiokaryo Tumulus|Q11505277|qid=Q11505277|1=Hioka Goryo Kofun}} (Harima Inahi Tarohime no Mikoto Hiokaryo) === Footnotes === Japanese book | last=Kaneko | first=Tetsu | authorlink=Tetsu Kaneko (historian) | title=Bunkan's activities in Higashiharima - Stone pagoda construction and cultivation land development centered on the blank 11 years - | journal=Kamakura Heritage Research | publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan | number=44 | pages=1–27 | year=2019 | month=10 | ref={{harvid|Kaneko|2019}} }} === Related Literature === * Yuzo Kanemoto, “Ritual Structure of Early Modern Local Shrines: ``Insumikago'' of Hyuga Daimyojin, Kako District, Harima Province, Historical World of Folklore (Mikage History Study Group Folklore Series 7), Iwata Shoin, 1994. * Yuzo Kanemoto, “Structure and Transformation of Early Modern Village Rituals (Mikage History Study Group Folklore Series 11)” Iwata Shoin, 1998. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kago (heavy cruiser)|Q1721897|qid=Q1721897|1=Japanese cruiser Kako}} - A spirit is sent to the ship's shrine. === External link === {{Commonscat|Hioka-jinja}} * [https://www.hiokajinja.jp/
